video_title: Who Killed the Montreal Expos? | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: Just finished watching this documentary on Netflix. I have to say, this is a consistent trend that has happened within multiple industries in Canada. What happened to the Montreal Expos was more than just greed, it was a multitude of poor decisions made, a crisis of economies to scale, lack of expertise and experience, as well as failed partnerships that sought the purchase of the team as a long term investment rather than the building of the brand and it's entities. It also did not help that as a province, Quebec was also going through it's own crisis of political corruption and sabotage. They left the documentary open ended but in my opinion, the real killer was not building the stadium and the province saw building the stadium as more of an optics issue than one for investment and cultural preservation.

video_title: Who Killed the Montreal Expos? | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
The Marlins should be relocated to Montreal based solely on their ongoing attendance issues. Montreal never received a new stadium, while Miami did — and despite that, their ballpark has remained mostly empty since it opened. The Marlins consistently rank near the bottom of Major League Baseball in attendance, often finishing 28th, 29th, or 30th out of 30 teams.

It’s been over 20 years since the Marlins last appeared in the World Series, and the franchise has been plagued by poor ownership. The organization shows little interest in retaining its star players or building long-term success in Miami.

Meanwhile, Montreal was treated unfairly when the Expos were taken away. It was a tragedy for baseball — the city and its fans deserved better. Jeffrey Loria, who later owned the Marlins, had no real commitment to keeping the team in Montreal and bought out the Quebec-based ownership group. At the time, Premier Lucien Bouchard refused to use provincial funds to build a new stadium, sealing the team’s fate.

If any team should move to Montreal, it’s the Rays — they lack a proper stadium and could thrive there. Call them the “Montreal ExRays.”

comment_text: Baseball actually has a long history in Montreal, dating back to the days of their minor league franchise, the first city Jackie Robinson played in that wasn't part of the Negro league. I remember watching the first game, when Rusty Staub and the boys took the field. I always had a bit of a soft spot for em and they were good at developing talent. One of the reasons I stayed away for so long after that jacked up strike in 94 was you had two teams, the Expos and the Indians, who were perennial losers that seemed to be on a collision course. What a World Series that would have been. When they took that away from the game, simply for the dollars, I was so pissed I left and stayed gone for a decade. I'll be curious to see this one. Like I say, I always had a soft spot for the Expos.

video_title: Who Killed the Montreal Expos? | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Easy Answer: Jeffery Loira

The Guy knew absolutely nothing about baseball and frankly didn’t care. All he cared was that if being in business was going make him money. What would later happen is the perfect example of how to completely destroy a franchise. First, he bought a stake of the team and used his business talk to slowly work up to the team’s president. Second, once the other shareholders couldn’t afford to stay with the team he bought the rest the stakes to become sole owner. Third, once he figured out that this game wasn’t gonna make him rich, he sells the team to MLB and in return they give him another team with a more rich market (The Florida-now Miami Marlins). Finally, He walks away with the money while the rest of Montreal watches as they say goodbye to their beloved team. This is the truly of the darkest stories in sports history.

comment_text: As a long-suffering Mets fan, I remember some great Expos teams. One of my clearest memories is that game at Shea Stadium in May 1987, when Tim Raines came back after a one-month holdout. He went 4-for-5 with two steals and capped it with a game-winning grand slam off Jesse Orosco. That team was written off before the season — they had just lost Andre Dawson and Jeff Reardon — but, like so many Expos clubs, they kept defying expectations and pushed the Mets and Cardinals right down to the wire. It’s a shame there’s no team in Montreal anymore. Vive les Expos!

video_title: Who Killed the Montreal Expos? | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: A few years after the demise of the Expos, I worked for a guy who grew up in Montreal. He felt that baseball in Montreal never took hold like in Toronto. There wasn't very much interest in Little League, very few high school baseball teams and what can you expect when the national summer sport is lacrosse. Fans can brag about the 1994 season but in 1995 they were 66 - 78. Dead last. In 1994 Blue Jays attendance was 2,907,933 while Expos was 1,276,250. In 1995 Blue jays attendance was 2,826,483 while Expos was 1,309,618. While there may have been shenanigans in the management, if your best years are constantly less than half the attendance of the team 5 hours away how can you not blame the fans?

comment_text: The Expo’s downfall feels like one where you can’t blame one single party; the 1994 strike killed whatever momentum the team would’ve gained had they continued on to the playoffs, Jeffrey Loria for being unable to keep any valuable assets, let alone those from the 1994 campaign, and to an extent, ownership politics keeping the Expos from moving out of the money-swallowing hellhole that was Stade Olympique. video_title: Who Killed the Montreal Expos? | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I was in Montreal for most of the 90s. Loria et al were terrible, but the stadium was the biggest problem.  Unless the team was killing it (and they were in 93 and 94) the place would be virtually empty, as it was a terrible place to see a game in many ways. If the team had more consistent support, an owner financing a new stadium themselves would be less risky.  As it was, they tried to extort the full bill for a new one near the Bell Centre from the province and the city.  The finances of both in the 90s were a nightmare. People wanted to blame Bouchard at the time but that was lunacy.  Quebec's credit rating was approaching junk status. The 90s was peak corporate welfare from southern US cities willing to pay for stadiums/arenas with huge tax breaks for the owners. Montreal was never going to be able to compete with that.

video_title: Who Killed the Montreal Expos? | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I was a huge fan since 1970.  Living in Ottawa, we made the 2-hour journey to Parc Jarry and then Olympic Stadium to catch Rusty, Gary Carter, Steve Rogers, Andre Dawson, Tim Raines, Tim Wallach, Pedro Martinez, Vlad Guerrero, Larry Walker, and others.  I only missed seeing games in Montréal in two years: 1969 and 2003.  I would listen to Dave Van Horne do the broadcasts.  I was completely invested in this team.  So many great memories.  When the Expos folded, I had such a bad taste in my mouth, especially regarding MLB itself, that I have not watched a single MLB game since.  That league is not getting a drop of my income or my time unless the Expos return.

video_title: Who Killed the Montreal Expos? | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I believe that the return of Montreal Alouettes to the CFL in 1996 marked the beginning of the end for the Expos in Montreal. For the first decade of the Expos existence in Montreal, the Alouettes were the talk of the town in the summer in Montreal. In the early 1980's, the Expos surpassed the Alouettes in popularity in Montreal and it led to the CFL not having a franchise there for a decade as the Alouettes folded. In 1996, the Alouettes returned to Montreal and became more popular than the Expos once again.

video_title: Who Killed the Montreal Expos? | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Omg, this look so good!!! 

I really hope this documentary goes all in & doesn’t whitewash what happened! There was a similar documentary on the MLB Network and they ended up having to cut like 10 minutes out of it that had to do with Loria. I saw the original version I was shocked that an MLB affiliated documentary put that in there… but secretly had to remove it! 

DC should’ve never gotten a team. It’s not a big enough market (I’m including Baltimore) to have two teams! It’s great for Phillies fans to have another home ballpark but it’s ridiculous they would add another team in that region. Look at what’s going on now, the O’s & Nats are still fighting over tv. 

Lastly, the Expos would still be in Montreal if there was revenue sharing like there is today. The whole thing is so sad. MLB, Loria, Selig and sports writers blamed the Expos fans & that was probably the most infuriating part of all of that saga!

video_title: Who Killed the Montreal Expos? | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Can't wait to see this.  As a kid in Connecticut, I was a Red Sox fan, but being a little OCD I needed a favorite team to root for in each division - in the NL East it was the Expos.  No idea why - I guess they seemed exotic in some way, and I had an old hand-me-down souvenir sweatshirt from Expo '67.  All I know is I loved Carter, Dawson, Valentine, Speier, Parrish, Rogers and the rest.  Blue Monday killed me only slightly less than Bucky Dent's HR in '78.  

I've been in DC since 1986, and while I was thrilled to get a club, I was heart sick is was the Expos.  There are many villains in the story of the Expos departure from Montreal, but make no mistake - this was no accident; it was a deliberate hit.  In my less lucid periods, I wonder whether the cancellation of the 1994 post season wasn't part of MLB's effort to stymie any chance the Expos had of being champions and to gain a wider following.  

One of the other commenters has it right - they need to do another one on the A's.
